task.title: Verify network interface status
task.description: Check active network interfaces and their operational state using system tools

solution.approach: Determine the operating system and available system tools to inspect network interface status. Common tools include `ip`, `ifconfig`, `netstat`, or `cat /proc/net/dev`. Prioritize using CLI commands over scripting to adhere to monitoring preference.
solution.algorithm: Check for presence of `ip` command first; fallback to `ifconfig` if not available. Avoid generating scripts unless necessary.

solution.approach: Design a diagnostic workflow that executes the appropriate network interface query command based on detected OS/tool availability. Capture stdout directly without file redirection.
solution.algorithm: Use conditional logic: if `which ip` succeeds, run `ip link show`; else if `which ifconfig` succeeds, run `ifconfig -a`. Output parsing is not required—raw output suffices for validation.

solution.approach: Execute the chosen network interface status command via `run_shell_command`. Use relative path awareness and avoid absolute paths or temporary files.
solution.algorithm: Run `ip link show` if available; otherwise execute `ifconfig -a`. Capture and return stdout directly.

solution.approach: Verify that the command executed successfully (exit code 0) and that the output contains expected fields such as interface names, states (UP/DOWN), and MAC addresses. No further processing needed—output confirms operational status.
solution.algorithm: Validate exit code and ensure non-empty stdout with recognizable interface entries (e.g., lines containing 'state', 'link/ether').

solution.approach: Determine the operating system and available system tools to inspect network interface status. Common tools include `ip`, `ifconfig`, `netstat`, or `cat /proc/net/dev`. Prioritize using CLI commands over scripting to adhere to monitoring preference.
solution.algorithm: Check for presence of `ip` command first; fallback to `ifconfig` if not available. Avoid generating scripts unless necessary.

task.title: Verify network interface status
task.description: Check active network interfaces and their operational state using system tools

artifacts.code: command -v ip && echo 'ip command available' || echo 'ip not found, checking ifconfig...'; command -v ifconfig && echo 'ifconfig available' || echo 'ifconfig not found'
if command -v ip &>/dev/null; then ip link show; else if command -v ifconfig &>/dev/null; then ifconfig; else cat /proc/net/dev; fi; fi

task.title: Test basic connectivity
task.description: Execute ping to a reliable external endpoint (e.g., 8.8.8.8) to confirm outbound connectivity

solution.approach: Analyze the task requirements to confirm the need for a basic connectivity test using ping to an external endpoint (e.g., 8.8.8.8). Determine that this is a straightforward diagnostic check and does not require complex logic or data processing.

Design the execution step to use the `ping` command with a count of 4 packets sent to 8.8.8.8, capturing output directly without file redirection. Ensure no absolute paths or system directories are used.
Execute the ping command using `run_shell_command` with arguments: ['ping', '-c', '4', '8.8.8.8']. This will send four ICMP echo requests and display the results in stdout.

Validate the success of the ping by checking if the command completed successfully (exit code 0) and that at least one packet was received. If the output contains '0% packet loss' or similar, consider connectivity confirmed.

Analyze the task requirements to confirm the need for a basic connectivity test using ping to an external endpoint (e.g., 8.8.8.8). Determine that this is a straightforward diagnostic check and does not require complex logic or data processing.

task.title: Assess DNS resolution capability
task.description: Validate ability to resolve domain names via DNS query using curl or ping to example.com

solution.approach: Begin by analyzing the system's DNS resolution capability using standard network diagnostic tools. Since the task involves validating DNS resolution for example.com, use curl with a DNS-over-TLS or plain HTTP request to test name resolution without downloading content. This avoids relying solely on ping, which may be disabled or misconfigured. The goal is to confirm that the system can resolve domain names via DNS.
solution.algorithm: Execute curl command targeting example.com with minimal headers to verify connectivity and DNS resolution. Capture response status and headers to determine if the domain resolves successfully.

solution.approach: Run a curl command against example.com using the --connect-timeout and --max-time options to limit execution time. Include verbose output to inspect DNS lookup details. Do not redirect output to any file; capture stdout directly for analysis.
solution.algorithm: Use run_shell_command to execute: curl -v --connect-timeout 5 --max-time 10 https://example.com

solution.approach: Validate the curl command output to confirm successful DNS resolution. Check for presence of 'Connected to' message indicating successful connection to example.com, and absence of errors related to name resolution (e.g., 'Could not resolve host'). If the command succeeds, DNS resolution is functional.
solution.algorithm: Parse stdout from step 2 for success indicators. If 'Connected to example.com' appears in output, mark DNS resolution as valid.

This avoids relying solely on ping, which may be disabled or misconfigured. The goal is to confirm that the system can resolve domain names via DNS.
solution.algorithm: Execute curl command targeting example.com with minimal headers to verify connectivity and DNS resolution. Capture response status and headers to determine if the domain resolves successfully.
